subject: Chemistry
options: Addition of an inert gas at constant pressure
Addition of an inert gas at constant volume
solution:
2 NO2g→ N2O4 g                ∆H=(-)ve
By addition of inert gas at constant pressure, volume increases and thus, the pressure decreases. The reaction now moves in backward direction as this would increase pressure and hence, decomposition of N2O4 increases.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_4(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \\ & \Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=-2\end{aligned}$
If pressure of system increases then according to Le-Chatelier's principle reaction will move in forward direction.
Concentration of reactant and products both increases but concentration of product increases more.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Initial pressure of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\frac{37.8}{108} \times 0.082 \times 500}{1}=14.35 \text { bar } \\
& 2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4+\mathrm{O}_2
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{t}=0 & 14.35 \\ \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{eq} & 14.35-2 \mathrm{P} & 2 \mathrm{P}\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \text { at eqb }=14.35+\mathrm{P}=18.65 \\ & \mathrm{P}=4.3 \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5}=5.75 \text { bar } \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4}=8.6 \mathrm{bar} \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}=4.3 \text { bar } \\ & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{(8.6)^2 \times(4.3)}{(5.75)^2}=9.619=\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2} \\ & \mathrm{x}=961.9 \approx 962\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The equilibrium constant of a chemical reaction (usually denoted by the symbol K) provides insight into the relationship between the products and reactants when a chemical reaction reaches equilibrium. For example, the equilibrium constant of concentration (denoted by Kc) of a chemical reaction at equilibrium can be defined as the ratio of the concentration of products to the concentration of the reactants, each raised to their respective stoichiometric coefficients. It is important to note that there are different types of equilibrium constants that provide relationships between the products and the reactants of equilibrium reactions in terms of different units.

Equilibrium Constant Formula

Keqb=KfKb=CcDdAaBb=Kc

Where Kc indicates the equilibrium constant measured in moles per litre.

KC=Products Reactants 

KC=FeSCN2+Fe3+SCN-

Hence, the answer is option A.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The Arrhenius equation in Logarithmic form:

In K=-∆HRT+ln A

On comparing with y=mx+c, m=-∆HR and c=ln A.

For an exothermic reaction, the value of ∆H<0, slope will be positive and the intercept can be either positive or negative.

solution:

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}}$
For the reaction
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \leftrightharpoons \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \Delta n_g=1-\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)=-\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore K_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{RT}}} ; \quad \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{R T}}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The equilibrium constant of concentration (denoted by Kc) of a chemical reaction at equilibrium can be defined as the ratio of the concentration of products to the concentration of the reactants, each raised to their respective stoichiometric coefficients.

The equilibrium reaction is 

N2g+3H2g⇋2NH3g

Kc=NH32H23N2

  =32×10-223×10-23×2×10-2

   =32×10-422×33×10-6×2×10-2

 =14×2×3×104=1000024=416.6≈417

Now, the equilibrium constant KC=CO2H2COH2O

⇒KC=0.04×0.040.06×0.06=0.44

∴Kc×102=44

solution: Hence $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{(2 x)^2 \times x}{(a-x)(1.5 a-2 x)^2}$ Given, at equilibrium $\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & (a-x)=(1.5 a-2 x) \\ \therefore & a=2 x\end{array}$ On solving $K_C=4$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
A      +      2B      ⇌       2C    +    D
Initial conc. of [A] = a , [B] = 1.5a , [C] = 0 and [D] = 0

At equilibrium [A] = (a - x) , [B] = 1.5(a - 2x) , [C] = 2x and [D] = x

At equilibrium a-x=1.5a-2x

0.5 a=x

a=2x

kC=2x2xa-x1.5a-2x2=4x2.xxx2=4

subject: Chemistry
options: the equilibrium will shift in the forward direction and more of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> gases will be produced.
the equilibrium will go backward due to suppression of dissociation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PCl</mi><mn>5</mn></msub></math>.
addition of helium will not affect the equilibrium.
solution:
PCI5( g)⇌PCl3( g)+Cl2( g)
In the above reaction vessel, when helium(an inert gas) is added following cases can happen for isobaric and isochoric processes respectively:

Case 1 : At constant P - volume will increase so reaction will shift in forward direction then answer will be A

Case 2 : At constant volume no change in active mass so reaction will not shift in any direction then answer will be D.

Thus, at constant volume, addition of helium will not have any impact on the equilibrium.

 n                                   Initial concentration

n-αn    αn    αn     at equilibrium
Let total pressure be p1

Using dalton's law of partial pressures and equation for equilibrium constant:
∴Kp1=α1+α×p121-α1+αp1

⇒3=α2×p11-α2       ....(X)

A(g)⇌2 B( g)  kp2=1

 x-αn  2αn     at equilibrium
Let total pressure be p2
Using dalton's law of partial pressures and equation for equilibrium constant

⇒31=p14p2

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$2 A B_3(g) \leftrightharpoons A_2(g)+3 B_2(g)$
at $t=0 \quad 8 \quad 0 \quad 0$
at eq. $(8-2 \times 2) \quad 2 \quad 3 \times 2$ $=4 \quad 2 \quad 6$
now $K_C=\frac{\left[A_2\right]\left[B_2\right]^3}{\left[A B_3\right]^2}=\frac{2 / 1 \times[6 / 1]^3}{[4 / 1]^2}=27$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The more is the value of equilibrium constant, the more is the completion of reaction or more is the concentration of products i.e. the order of relative strength would be
$$
\mathrm{OH}^{-}>\mathrm{CN}^{-}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$
